did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes 'MelDetect', a lightweight and interpretable deep learning model for melanoma classification from dermoscopic images. It utilizes an EfficientNetV2 CNN backbone for feature extraction and classification, combined with the Aquila Optimizer (a nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithm) for optimizing model parameters. The study was conducted using the ISIC 2019 dataset of dermoscopic images to classify skin lesions into melanoma, nevus, and other categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents 'MelDetect', a novel lightweight and interpretable deep learning model for melanoma classification. It details a specific methodology using EfficientNetV2 and the Aquila Optimizer, describes the experimental setup using the ISIC 2019 dataset, and reports original quantitative results (precision 0.92, recall 0.83, F1-score 0.87). Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original experimental validation and algorithmic contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: MelDetect: A Lightweight and Interpretable Model for Melanoma Classification From Dermoscopic Images
journal: Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT-2026)
brief_summary: This study proposes MelDetect, a lightweight and interpretable deep learning model for classifying melanoma from dermoscopic images. The architecture utilizes an EfficientNetV2 CNN backbone for feature extraction and the Aquila Optimizer for parameter optimization, eliminating the need for separate segmentation modules. The model was trained and evaluated using a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset, specifically focusing on three classes: melanoma, nevus, and others. Experimental results demonstrated strong performance, achieving an accuracy of 0.95, precision of 0.94, and an F1-score of 0.93. The proposed method outperformed comparative approaches such as CNN+GWO, ANFIS+FFNN, and SNC_Net in key classification metrics. These findings suggest MelDetect is a viable, low-computational-footprint tool for clinical decision support in melanoma screening.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, and Others/BCC/AKIEC/DF/VASC/BKL)
all_task_types.details: The model classifies dermoscopic images into 3 discrete classes: melanoma, nevus, and others. It uses a softmax activation function to produce a probability distribution over these classes and predicts the class with the maximum projected probability. Performance is evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset, an open-source repository of high-resolution dermoscopic images with expert-annotated diagnostic labels.
classes_used: Others (consolidated class including BCC, AKIEC, DF, VASC, and BKL)

use.training: Used for training the MelDetect model with data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flip, rotation, brightness/contrast jitter, zoom).
use.validation: Used for monitoring generalization and loss during training (validation loss stabilized at 0.4).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of classification performance (precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons with other models (CNN+GWO, ANFIS+FFNN, SNC_Net) were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, F1-score) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2019.notes: The model was trained for 25 epochs with a batch size of 32 using categorical cross-entropy loss. Training accuracy increased from 70% to 91%. Validation loss stabilized at 0.4. The paper states MelDetect achieved superior performance compared to CNN+GWO, ANFIS+FFNN, and SNC_Net with 95% accuracy, 94% precision, and 93% F1-score. In the melanoma category specifically, it achieved 0.92 precision, 0.83 recall, and 0.87 F1-score. The confusion matrix showed 250 correct melanoma predictions, 410 correct nevus predictions, and 210 correct 'others' predictions.

architecture_details.backbone_features: Fused-MBConv blocks for multi-scale feature learning
architecture_details.optimizer_update_rule: Dynamic update rule balancing global exploration and local exploitation

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GTX 1080Ti GPU, 64GB storage, high RAM

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected dense layer with softmax activation

conclusions: The proposed MelDetect model, which combines an EfficientNetV2 backbone with the Aquila Optimizer, achieves superior classification performance compared to comparative methods such as CNN+GWO, ANFIS+FFNN, and SNC_Net.
MelDetect demonstrates strong overall classification performance with 95% accuracy, 94% precision, and 93% F1-score on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
In the specific melanoma category, the model achieved a precision of 0.92, recall of 0.83, and an F1-score of 0.87.
The model eliminates the need for a separate segmentation module by utilizing an end-to-end learning approach, thereby reducing computational overhead.
MelDetect maintains a low computational footprint, making it suitable for real-time or mobile deployment in low-resource clinical environments.

The study addresses limitations of traditional diagnostic methods and conventional deep learning approaches, such as high computation requirements and data imbalance, by providing a lightweight and interpretable solution.

The model showed good generalization capabilities with no significant overfitting issues, as indicated by the close alignment of training and validation accuracy curves (reaching 91% and 90% respectively) and a stabilized validation loss of 0.4.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which limits the assessment of external validity and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The 'Others' class is a consolidated group containing multiple distinct pathologies (BCC, AKIEC, DF, VASC, BKL), which masks class-specific performance and may obscure diagnostic errors for specific lesion types.

The dataset split is highly imbalanced, with the 'Others' class comprising only 100 training samples (1000 testing samples) compared to 1200 melanoma and 1800 nevus training samples, potentially biasing the model against the minority class.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of MelDetect over comparative models; comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The paper claims the model is 'interpretable' but does not provide specific interpretability visualizations (e.g., Grad-CAM, SHAP) or quantitative explainability metrics to support this claim.

The model was trained using the Adam optimizer for the final classification head, while the Aquila Optimizer was used to optimize the EfficientNetV2 backbone weights; the interaction between these two optimization strategies is not fully detailed.
The paper acknowledges that low recall for melanoma (0.83) leads to misclassification of melanoma cases as nevus, indicating a risk of false negatives in clinical screening applications.

The study is a conference paper published in 2026, which may have undergone less rigorous peer review compared to high-impact journals, and lacks independent clinical validation or deployment in real-world settings.
